feed.title: napkin — blog
feed.subtitle: Posts on Swift 6 concurrency, iOS app architecture, dependency injection, actor testing, and modular iOS apps.

entry.title: The Swift 6 @MainActor listener-conformance error, and a seam that sidesteps it
entry.summary: "Main actor-isolated property 'listener' cannot be used to satisfy nonisolated protocol requirement" is the error every RIB-shaped listener seam hits under Swift 6. Why it happens, and the whole-protocol @MainActor design that makes it structurally impossible.

entry.title: A RIBs alternative for Swift Concurrency, without RxSwift: meet napkin
entry.summary: Uber's RIBs is alive, well, and actively developed. It's also built on RxSwift and ships a runtime leak detector. napkin is the same Router-Interactor-Builder pattern written for Swift Concurrency, without either — Clean Architecture on a napkin.

entry.title: Actor isolation for iOS app architecture in Swift 6
entry.summary: Swift 6 makes you declare where every piece of code runs. That's an architecture decision in disguise. Here's the four-ring isolation map napkin uses and why business logic belongs off the main actor.

entry.title: Dependency injection in SwiftUI without third-party libraries
entry.summary: SwiftUI's @Environment wasn't designed for services. @EnvironmentObject crashes at runtime. There's a third option that's been around since RIBs and works cleanly with Swift 6 actors.

entry.title: Testing Swift actors: a practical guide
entry.summary: If your business logic is in final actor types, you can unit-test it without a simulator. Mock the protocols, drive the lifecycle, assert on recorded calls.

entry.title: Modular iOS apps with Swift Concurrency
entry.summary: Modularity isn't a build-system question. It's about whether features can be reasoned about in isolation. Here's how to compose an iOS app as a tree of small, isolated units in Swift 6.
